exemplar.title: What is the relationship between water temperature and the angle of refraction of light?

detailedExemplarData.title: What is the relationship between water temperature and the angle of refraction of light?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Graph presentation is clear and includes error bars, enhancing visual precision.
Trendline equation is provided, allowing quantitative interpretation of the relationship.
Uncertainties in measurements are justified based on instrument precision and displayed on the graph.
Data recording and processing steps are communicated clearly on the graph, achieving both clarity and reasonable precision.
weaknesses: Rawâdata table has misaligned headings and inconsistent unit formatting, reducing overall clarity.
No propagation of uncertainties through the gradient calculation to quantify slope error.
Criteria for outlier exclusion are undocumented; statistical basis (e.g., >2Ï rule) is needed.
Analysis does not compute refractive index vs temperature or compare to theoretical values, limiting depth.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is both clear and reasonably precise on the graph (band 5), but formatting issues in the table and omissions in statistical treatment keep it from the highest band. Consideration of uncertainties is appropriate but lacks propagation (band 4), and data processing has significant omissions (band 3â4). Overall, a holistic score of 4 accurately reflects these strengths and limitations.

strengths: Research question articulated within a specific, appropriate context with links to realâworld applications.
Rich background on Snellâs law, densityâtemperature relationship and practical photography implications.
Detailed, logically structured procedure with equipment list, uncertainties and annotated diagrams facilitating reproducibility.
Clear identification of independent, dependent and control variables, demonstrating sound methodological planning.
Instrument uncertainties provided enhance transparency and repeatability.
weaknesses: Volume of water and exact cameraâtoâcontainer distances are omitted, risking reproducibility lapses.
No quantitative expectation (predicted gradient) is specified to strengthen theoretical context.
Rationale for the chosen 20â50 Â°C range and interval selection is not fully justified relative to measurement sensitivity.
Variables in Snellâs law equation are not explicitly defined, reducing clarity in theoretical presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates an excellent, specific context for the research question (band 5) and a procedure that can be reproduced with only minor ambiguities (band 4). Methodological considerations are well described, though explanations of trial number and quantitative precision remain limited (band 4). The overall performance aligns with the top of the 3â4 band and meets several descriptors of band 5, warranting a holistic score of 5.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly summarizes the positive correlation and ties back to the original hypothesis.
Discussion references the underlying physics of density decrease and refractive index change, linking to results.
The gradient of the line is quoted, demonstrating engagement with quantitative findings.
weaknesses: No quantified uncertainty on the gradient is provided, reducing rigor of the final claim.
Comparison with literature values or theoretical refractive-indexâtemperature data is absent.
Impact of excluded data points on the conclusion is not discussed, limiting completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is described and largely consistent with the analysis (band 3â4), providing relevant physics context. It remains partially justified due to lack of uncertainty quantification and deeper literature comparison, placing it solidly in the 3â4 band and meriting a score of 4.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (container wall refraction, camera angle bias, protractor scale) are identified and their impacts on accuracy and precision are explained.
Realistic improvements (thinner walls, top-entry beam, finer angle measurement, extended temperature range) directly address identified limitations.
Each suggested improvement is clearly linked to a specific weakness, demonstrating coherent explanatory depth.
Proposed refinements are feasible and relevant, showing a clear path to enhancing data quality and validity.
weaknesses: Suggestions are not prioritized by feasibility or expected accuracy gain, making implementation planning less clear.
No quantitative estimate of the relative contribution of each limitation to overall uncertainty.
Ranking or phasing of improvements by costâbenefit is not provided.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation explains the relative impact of specific methodological weaknesses (band 5â6) and offers realistic, relevant improvements with clear explanations (band 5â6). The depth and clarity of both strands meet the highest band descriptors, justifying a score of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated and clearly linked to a realâworld phenomenon (optics in water of varying temperature) and to underlying physics concepts (density dependence of refractive index).  Background information on Snellâs law, densityâtemperature graphs and practical applications (sea photography) provides a specific, appropriate context rather than a generic one.  Minor gaps (e.g., no mention of quantitative expected gradient) do not detract from the overall specificity.

Uncertainties in both measured variables are stated, justified and displayed on the graph.  No propagation, percentage uncertainty or discussion of gradient uncertainty is attempted, so several significant omissions remain.  This aligns with the middle band where some consideration is evident but incomplete.

Methodological considerations such as control of container, wavelength, angle of incidence, and assumptions about uniform temperature and n_air are described.  However, justification of data range, number of trials, and quantitative link to required precision is limited.  The considerations are therefore described but not fully explained, fitting mid-band performance.

Specific weaknesses (plastic wall refraction, camera angle, coarse protractor scale, limited temperature range) are described and their qualitative impact on accuracy and precision is mentioned (e.g., larger uncertainty, possible distortion).  The explanation of relative impact is brief and not prioritised, placing it solidly in the 3â4 mark band.

The student supplies a step-by-step procedure, equipment list with uncertainties, safety notes and annotated photographs/diagrams.  A reader could reproduce the investigation with only a few ambiguities (exact camera height, distance of laser, volume of water).  Those minor omissions keep the work from the highest band but still meet the descriptor for reproducibility with few ambiguities.

Data processing is limited to plotting points and fitting straight lines, with subjective exclusion of outliers and no calculation of refractive index or use of Snellâs law to predict values.  The omission of repeat trials and statistical treatment constitutes major omissions, matching the 1â2 mark descriptor; some processing is present so a score of 2 is appropriate.

Several realistic, relevant improvements are proposedâusing thinner walls or top-entry beam, more precise angle measurement via beam trace, extending temperature range with ice and high-temperature apparatusâand each is linked to the limitation it tackles, showing some explanatory depth.  While not exhaustive or prioritised, the reasoning is clear enough to merit the top of the 3â4 band.

A raw-data table and a clearly labelled graph with error bars are presented; trendlines and equations are given.  Formatting issues in the table (misaligned headings, mixed symbols) reduce precision, but overall communication is clear enough for the reader and largely precise on the graph, satisfying the âeither clear OR preciseâ descriptor.

There is a qualitative comparison to accepted physics: density decreases with temperature, refractive index falls, hence refraction angle increases.  This comparison is relevant and partially linked to the results but lacks quantitative reference to literature values or Snellâs law calculations, so the justification remains partial rather than thorough.

The student states and discusses a linear relationship that matches the plotted data and refers back to the hypothesis.  While the gradient is quoted, the uncertainty of the gradient and effect of ignored points are not analysed, so the conclusion is not âfullyâ consistent or rigorously justified, placing it in the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: Consider specifying an expected quantitative relationship, such as the predicted slope of the refractionâtemperature graph based on theoretical refractive index models, to strengthen context.
The introduction provides a rich historical and personal context by discussing Snellâs discovery and the authorâs own experiences, effectively situating the investigation.
The stepâbyâstep procedure is logically structured and clear, facilitating accurate repetition of the experiment.
Details on water volume and camera distance are omitted; specify these parameters in the method to ensure reproducibility.
The research question is clearly articulated and framed within a specific context, linking water temperature effects to refraction and practical applications in sea photography.
Clarify the variable definitions in Snellâs law equation $$\sin\theta_r=\frac{\sin\theta_i\,n_i}{n_r}$$ by explicitly stating which index refers to incident and refracted media.
Linking the densityâtemperature graph to refractive index changes demonstrates solid theoretical understanding and justifies the hypothesis.
Consider including quantitative predictions for refractive index change with temperature, based on literature, to make the theoretical context more rigorous.
The hypothesis is well articulated, linking warmer water to faster light speed, lower refractive index, and larger refraction angles.
Excellent identification of independent, dependent, and control variables, demonstrating clear methodological planning.
The rationale for choosing a 20â50 Â°C range is not fully justified; explain why these bounds and intervals were selected relative to expected measurement sensitivity.
Providing instrument uncertainties (e.g., Â±0.5 Â°C thermometer, Â±0.5Â° protractor) significantly enhances the reproducibility of the method.
Include annotations on the experimental diagram (Fig. 4) for distances such as camera height and laserâtoâcontainer spacing to aid replication.
The raw data table has misaligned headings and inconsistent formatting of units, which diminishes clarity.
Uncertainties are justified based on instrument precision and measurement conditions, demonstrating careful consideration of error sources.
Propagate these measurement uncertainties through the gradient calculation to quantify the uncertainty in the slope.
Calculate refractive index vs temperature and compare to theoretical expectations to strengthen data interpretation.
Consider ranking suggested improvements by feasibility and expected accuracy gains to create a clear plan for refinement.
Criteria for excluding outliers are not documented; define a statistical basis (e.g., >2Ï deviation) for outlier removal.
The graph includes error bars for temperature uncertainty, enhancing the visual precision of the data presentation.
Presenting the fitted trendline equation $$y=0.0448x+27.2$$ allows direct quantitative interpretation of the relationship.
Include the coefficient of determination (RÂ²) for the linear fit to assess goodness of fit and model validity.
Data processing lacks calculation of refractive index variation $$n(T)$$ using Snellâs law; adding this would deepen the analysis.
The conclusion succinctly summarizes the positive correlation and ties back to the hypothesis, maintaining relevance to the research question.
The conclusion lacks a quantified uncertainty on the gradient; include the margin of error in the final statement to enhance rigor.
Integrate quantitative literature data for refractive index vs temperature to strengthen comparison with accepted scientific context.
Specific methodological weaknesses are identified, such as container wall refraction and camera angle, showing critical evaluation.
Estimate the relative impact of each limitation (e.g., % error from plastic wall vs. angle measurement) to prioritize which to address first.
Proposed improvements (thinner walls, beam entry from above, precise beam measurements) are realistic and directly target identified issues.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
